examples.title: Bresenham's Line Algorithm
examples.description: Efficient integer arithmetic line drawing algorithm for raster displays
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BresenhamLine(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
    dx = x2 - x1
    dy = y2 - y1
    
    IF dx = 0 THEN
        PlotVerticalLine(x1, y1, y2)
        RETURN
    END IF
    
    x = x1
    y = y1
    
    IF dx > dy THEN
        # Shallow line (|slope| <= 1)
        P = 2*dy - dx  # Initial decision parameter
        
        WHILE x <= x2 DO
            PlotPixel(x, y, color)
            
            IF P < 0 THEN
                P = P + 2*dy
                x = x + 1
            ELSE
                P = P + 2*dy - 2*dx
                x = x + 1
                y = y + 1
            END IF
        END WHILE
    ELSE
        # Steep line (|slope| > 1)
        # Swap roles of x and y
        P = 2*dx - dy
        
        WHILE y <= y2 DO
            PlotPixel(x, y, color)
            
            IF P < 0 THEN
                P = P + 2*dx
                y = y + 1
            ELSE
                P = P + 2*dx - 2*dy
                y = y + 1
                x = x + 1
            END IF
        END WHILE
    END IF
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm uses a decision parameter P to determine whether the next pixel should be placed at the same y-coordinate or at an incremented y-coordinate. For lines with |slope| ≤ 1, it steps horizontally; for steeper lines, it steps vertically. Only integer operations are used, making it highly efficient.

examples.title: DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) Algorithm
examples.description: Line drawing algorithm using incremental calculation based on slope
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DDA(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
    dx = x2 - x1
    dy = y2 - y1
    
    steps = MAX(ABS(dx), ABS(dy))
    
    xInc = dx / steps  # x increment per step
    yInc = dy / steps  # y increment per step
    
    x = x1
    y = y1
    
    FOR i = 0 TO steps DO
        PlotPixel(ROUND(x), ROUND(y), color)
        x = x + xInc
        y = y + yInc
    END FOR
END
examples.explanation: DDA calculates the number of steps based on the maximum difference in x or y. It then increments x and y by fractional amounts and rounds to the nearest integer for plotting. While conceptually simpler than Bresenham's, it uses floating-point arithmetic which is slower.

examples.title: Boundary Fill Algorithm
examples.description: Recursive algorithm to fill an enclosed region bounded by a specific color
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BoundaryFill(x, y, fillColor, boundaryColor)
BEGIN
    currentColor = GetPixel(x, y)
    
    # Check if current pixel is not boundary and not already filled
    IF currentColor ≠ boundaryColor AND currentColor ≠ fillColor THEN
        SetPixel(x, y, fillColor)
        
        # Recursively fill 4-connected neighbors
        BoundaryFill(x + 1, y, fillColor, boundaryColor)
        BoundaryFill(x - 1, y, fillColor, boundaryColor)
        BoundaryFill(x, y + 1, fillColor, boundaryColor)
        BoundaryFill(x, y - 1, fillColor, boundaryColor)
    END IF
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm starts from a seed point and recursively fills all 4-connected pixels that are not the boundary color. It stops when it encounters the boundary or already-filled pixels. Can be implemented iteratively to avoid stack overflow for large regions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of pixels filled

examples.title: Scan-line Polygon Fill Algorithm
examples.description: Efficient algorithm for filling polygons using horizontal scan lines
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ScanLineFill(polygon)
BEGIN
    # Build Edge Table
    edgeTable = []
    FOR each edge (y1, y2, x1, x2) in polygon DO
        IF y1 = y2 THEN  # Skip horizontal edges
            CONTINUE
        END IF
        
        # Calculate inverse slope
        x = MIN(x1, x2)  # x of lower endpoint
        invSlope = (x2 - x1) / (y2 - y1)
        
        Add to edgeTable at index MIN(y1, y2):
            (yMax, x, invSlope)
    END FOR
    
    y = 0
    activeList = []
    
    WHILE y < screenHeight DO
        # Add edges starting at this scan line
        FOR each edge in edgeTable[y] DO
            Add edge to activeList
        END FOR
        
        # Remove edges that end at this line
        REMOVE edges with yMax = y from activeList
        
        # Sort active list by x-coordinate
        SORT activeList by x
        
        # Fill between pairs of intersections
        FOR i = 0 TO activeList.length-1 STEP 2 DO
            x1 = activeList[i].x
            x2 = activeList[i+1].x
            FillPixels(x1, x2, y)
        END FOR
        
        # Update x values for next scan line
        FOR each edge in activeList DO
            edge.x = edge.x + edge.invSlope
        END FOR
        
        y = y + 1
    END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm uses an edge table to store all polygon edges sorted by their lower y-coordinate. For each scan line, it maintains an active edge list containing only edges that intersect that line. Pairs of intersection points define the fill intervals. The algorithm is efficient for complex polygons and is commonly used in graphics hardware.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n + h) where n = edges, h = scan lines
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for edge table and active list
